title: US: Civil Construction Induction
subtitle: Entry-Level Safety and Compliance — United States
description: Essential OSHA knowledge for civil construction workers in the United States. Covers 29 CFR 1926, competent person requirements, excavation safety, crane operations, and MUTCD traffic control standards.

lessons.title: OSHA 29 CFR 1926 — Inspections, Violations and Worker Rights
lessons.content:
## OSHA and the Construction Industry

The Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA) is the primary federal agency responsible for ensuring safe and healthful working conditions in the United States. For construction, the governing standard is **29 CFR Part 1926** — Safety and Health Regulations for Construction.

## OSHA's Authority and Purpose

OSHA was established by the **Occupational Safety and Health Act of 1970** (OSH Act). Its mandate:
- Set and enforce workplace safety and health standards
- Provide training, outreach, education, and compliance assistance
- Work with states to develop their own OSHA plans

**Coverage:** The OSH Act covers most private sector employers and workers in all 50 states. Federal, state, and local government workers are covered under separate programs.

## 29 CFR 1926 — Construction Standards Structure

| Subpart | Topic |
|---------|-------|
| Subpart C | General Safety and Health Provisions |
| Subpart E | Personal Protective and Life Saving Equipment |
| Subpart K | Electrical |
| Subpart L | Scaffolds |
| Subpart M | Fall Protection |
| Subpart P | Excavations |
| Subpart Q | Concrete and Masonry |
| Subpart W | Rollover Protective Structures (ROPS) |
| Subpart CC | Cranes and Derricks |

## OSHA Inspections

OSHA compliance officers (inspectors) conduct workplace inspections:

**Types of inspections:**
- **Imminent danger**: Highest priority — conditions that could cause death or serious harm immediately
- **Fatality/catastrophe**: Following a work-related death or hospitalization of 3+ workers
- **Complaint**: Worker or third-party complaint filed with OSHA
- **Referral**: From another agency or media report
- **Follow-up**: Verify previous violation was corrected
- **Planned/programmed**: Based on injury/illness rate targeting high-hazard industries

**Inspection process:**
1. OSHA inspector arrives — presents credentials
2. Opening conference — explains scope of inspection
3. Walkthrough inspection — photographs, measurements, interviews
4. Closing conference — preliminary findings discussed
5. Citations issued (if violations found) within 6 months

## OSHA Violations and Penalties

| Violation Type | Description | Penalty (2024) |
|---------------|-------------|----------------|
| **Other-than-serious** | Violation with no direct death/serious injury risk | Up to $16,131 per violation |
| **Serious** | Substantial probability of death or serious injury | Up to $16,131 per violation |
| **Willful** | Employer knew about hazard and did not fix it | $11,524–$161,323 per violation |
| **Repeat** | Same violation found within 5 years | Up to $161,323 per violation |
| **Failure to abate** | Not fixing a cited violation | Up to $16,131 per day beyond deadline |

## Worker Rights Under OSHA

Workers have the following rights:
- **Right to a safe workplace**: OSHA protection from known hazards
- **Right to information**: Access to OSHA standards, injury/illness records (OSHA 300 logs), SDS
- **Right to training**: In a language and vocabulary workers understand
- **Right to file a complaint**: Confidential complaint to OSHA without employer retaliation
- **Right to participate in inspections**: Accompany the OSHA inspector
- **Anti-retaliation protection**: Section 11(c) of OSH Act prohibits employer retaliation for exercising safety rights

## State OSHA Plans

25 states and territories operate their own OSHA-approved state plans:
- Must be at least as effective as federal OSHA
- Cover both private and public sector workers (federal OSHA does not cover public sector)
- Examples: Cal/OSHA (California), WISHA (Washington State), Michigan OSHA

## Key Takeaways

- OSHA: established 1970 OSH Act — 29 CFR 1926 governs construction
- Inspections: imminent danger highest priority; planned inspections target high-hazard industries
- Willful violations: up to $161,323 per violation — employer knew about hazard and failed to act
- Workers: right to information, complaint, inspection participation — anti-retaliation protected
- 25 states have their own OSHA plans — must be at least as effective as federal OSHA

questions.q: What is the primary OSHA standard governing construction safety in the United States?
questions.explanation: 29 CFR Part 1926 is the Safety and Health Regulations for Construction — the primary OSHA standard for all construction work. 29 CFR 1910 applies to general industry workplaces.

questions.q: A worker files a confidential safety complaint with OSHA. What protections does the worker have?
options: No specific protections — the employer may take disciplinary action
Section 11(c) of the OSH Act prohibits employer retaliation for exercising OSHA rights
Protection only if the complaint results in a citation
questions.explanation: Section 11(c) of the OSH Act prohibits employer retaliation against workers for filing safety complaints, participating in inspections, or exercising any OSHA rights. Retaliation complaints can be filed with OSHA.

questions.q: An employer is cited for a willful OSHA violation — the employer knew about the hazard and failed to correct it. What is the maximum penalty per violation?
questions.explanation: Willful violations carry penalties of $11,524–$161,323 per violation (2024 figures). Willful = employer knew about the hazard and intentionally failed to correct it. These are the highest OSHA civil penalties.

questions.q: What is the highest priority type of OSHA inspection?
questions.explanation: Imminent danger inspections are OSHA's highest priority — these are conditions that could cause death or serious physical harm immediately. OSHA attempts to inspect these within 24 hours of notification.

questions.q: State OSHA plans must be:
options: At least as effective as federal OSHA, and they cover public sector workers that federal OSHA does not
Less strict than federal OSHA to allow local flexibility
questions.explanation: State OSHA plans must be at least as effective as federal OSHA. A key advantage is they cover both private AND public sector workers — federal OSHA does not cover state/local government workers directly.

lessons.title: 29 CFR 1926.502 — Guardrails, Safety Nets and Personal Fall Arrest
lessons.content:
## OSHA Fall Protection — 29 CFR 1926 Subpart M

Falls are the leading cause of fatalities in construction — consistently accounting for 30–40% of construction deaths annually. OSHA 29 CFR 1926 Subpart M establishes comprehensive fall protection requirements for construction.

## When is Fall Protection Required?

Fall protection is required for any worker at risk of falling **6 feet or more** to a lower level (1926.501):
- Unprotected sides and edges
- Floor and roof openings
- Holes (floor openings, manholes, skylights)
- Formwork and reinforcing steel
- Excavation edges

**Note:** Scaffolds have a lower trigger — 10 feet under Subpart L. Ladders: different requirements under Subpart X.

## The Three Accepted Methods of Fall Protection

OSHA accepts three main systems (1926.502):

### 1. Guardrail Systems
- **Top rail**: 42 inches (±3 inches) above walking/working surface
- **Mid rail**: 21 inches above the surface (halfway between top rail and surface)
- **Toe board**: 3.5 inches minimum height — prevents tools/material from falling
- **Strength**: Top rail must withstand 200 lb force outward or downward
- **Openings**: Mid rail openings must not pass a 19-inch sphere

### 2. Safety Net Systems
- Installed as close as practicable below the walking/working surface — no more than 30 feet below
- Must extend outward from outermost projection of work surface (8–13 feet depending on height)
- Drop-tested within 6 months before use
- Debris and tools must be removed promptly

### 3. Personal Fall Arrest Systems (PFAS)
- **Components**: Full-body harness + shock-absorbing lanyard/SRL + anchorage point
- **Anchorage**: Must support minimum 5,000 lb per attached worker
- **Free fall limit**: Maximum 6 feet of free fall
- **Deceleration distance**: Maximum 3.5 feet after activating the shock absorber
- **Total fall arrest distance**: Must ensure worker does not contact lower level

**Harness requirements:**
- Full-body harness only (body belts no longer permitted for fall arrest — only for positioning)
- Inspect before each use — discard any harness that has arrested a fall
- Proper fit — snug at shoulders, chest, and legs

## Fall Protection Plan

When conventional fall protection (guardrails/nets/PFAS) is infeasible or creates a greater hazard, a written Fall Protection Plan (FPP) may be used — but only in limited circumstances:
- Residential construction (specific provisions)
- Certain leading-edge work
- Must be prepared by a qualified person
- Must be site-specific

## Controlled Decking Zone (CDZ) and Leading Edges

For structural steel erection work, OSHA allows a Controlled Decking Zone — a designated area where up to 90 sq ft of unsupported decking can be placed without immediate fall protection, with strict access controls.

## Hole Covers

Floor/roof holes must be covered or guarded:
- Covers must support twice the maximum intended load
- Must be secured to prevent accidental displacement
- Must be marked 'HOLE' or 'COVER' to alert workers

## Key Takeaways

- 6-foot trigger for most construction fall protection — falls are #1 construction killer
- Three accepted methods: guardrails (42-inch top rail), safety nets (within 30 ft below), PFAS
- PFAS: full-body harness, 5,000 lb anchorage, max 6 ft free fall, discard after arresting a fall
- Body belts are NOT permitted for fall arrest — full-body harness required
- Hole covers: secured, marked, rated for twice the maximum load

questions.q: At what height must OSHA fall protection be provided for most construction activities?
questions.explanation: OSHA requires fall protection when workers are at risk of falling 6 feet or more to a lower level for most construction activities under 29 CFR 1926.501. Scaffolds have a 10-foot trigger under Subpart L.

questions.q: What is the required height of the top rail in an OSHA-compliant guardrail system?
questions.explanation: OSHA requires the top rail to be 42 inches (plus or minus 3 inches) above the walking/working surface. The mid rail must be approximately 21 inches — halfway between the top rail and the floor.

questions.q: An anchorage point for a personal fall arrest system must be able to support:
questions.explanation: OSHA requires anchorage points for PFAS to support a minimum of 5,000 pounds per attached worker. This must be verified by a qualified person — not assumed based on the attachment structure.

questions.q: A worker's personal fall arrest system has just arrested a fall. What must happen to the harness?
options: Inspect it and return to service if no visible damage
Return to service after a 30-day rest period
Remove from service immediately and discard or return to manufacturer
questions.explanation: Any harness or PFAS component that has arrested a fall must be immediately removed from service and discarded or returned to the manufacturer for inspection. The arrest forces can damage the harness internally without visible signs.

questions.q: Body belts (safety belts) are permitted under OSHA 1926.502 for:
options: Fall arrest — they are equivalent to full-body harnesses
Positioning work only — NOT for fall arrest
questions.explanation: Body belts are no longer permitted for fall arrest — they can cause internal injuries if they arrest a fall. They are allowed only for positioning systems (keeping workers in place while working). Full-body harnesses are required for fall arrest.

lessons.title: 29 CFR 1926.650-652 — Soil Classification, Shoring and Benching
lessons.content:
## OSHA Excavation Standards — 29 CFR 1926 Subpart P

Excavation collapse is the second leading cause of construction fatalities. OSHA's Subpart P (29 CFR 1926.650–652) establishes requirements for excavation safety on all US construction sites.

## Definitions

- **Excavation**: Any man-made cut, cavity, trench, or depression in an earth surface formed by earth removal
- **Trench**: A narrow excavation (width ≤ 15 feet) made below the surface of the ground (depth > width)
- **Competent person**: One who identifies existing and predictable hazards, AND has authority to take prompt corrective measures

**A competent person must be present on site during ALL excavation work.** This is non-negotiable.

## When is Protection Required?

Protective systems are required for ALL excavations 5 feet or deeper. Excavations less than 5 feet may also require protection if the competent person determines unstable soil is present.

## Soil Classification — Appendix B to Subpart P

| Class | Description | Maximum Allowable Slope |
|-------|-------------|------------------------|
| **Stable Rock** | Natural solid mineral matter — can be excavated with vertical sides | Vertical (90°) |
| **Type A** | Cohesive soil — unconfined compressive strength ≥ 1.5 tsf. Clay, silty clay | 3/4:1 (53°) |
| **Type B** | Cohesive soil — unconfined strength 0.5–1.5 tsf. Silt, sandy loam | 1:1 (45°) |
| **Type C** | Cohesive soil — unconfined strength < 0.5 tsf. Granular soils, sand, gravel, submerged soil | 1.5:1 (34°) |

**Important:** Soil cannot be classified as Type A if:
- Previously disturbed or fissured
- Subject to seeping water or vibration
- Material is part of a sloped, layered system
- Subject to other factors that would affect stability

**Soil classification tests (Appendix B):**
- Visual test: fissures, tension cracks, water seepage, layering
- Thumb test: penetration resistance of the soil
- Pocket penetrometer: measures unconfined compressive strength
- Torvane shear device

## Three Protective System Options

### 1. Sloping
Cut the excavation walls at or shallower than the maximum allowable slope for the soil type. Most economical for wide, shallow excavations with space to slope.

### 2. Shoring
Install a support system preventing soil movement:
- Timber shoring (Appendix C tables)
- Aluminum hydraulic shoring (Appendix D tables)
- Pneumatic/hydraulic trench shields
- Must be designed by a registered professional engineer for complex excavations

### 3. Trench Shield/Box (Trench Box)
A portable shield system providing worker protection if collapse occurs:
- Does NOT prevent collapse — protects the worker inside
- Workers must work within the protected zone of the shield
- Shield must be sized for the depth and soil conditions
- Must not be overloaded by placing equipment on top

## Other Key Requirements

- **Spoil placement**: Minimum 2 feet from excavation edge
- **Access/egress**: Ladder or ramp within 25 feet of workers in excavations > 4 feet deep
- **Underground utilities**: Contact 811 (Call Before You Dig) before excavating
- **Atmospheric hazards**: Test for hazardous atmospheres in excavations > 4 feet deep where atmosphere could be hazardous (confined space precautions)
- **Water accumulation**: Remove water before workers enter; monitor continuously
- **Daily inspection**: Competent person inspects excavation, adjacent areas, and protective systems every shift

## Key Takeaways

- Competent person required on site for ALL excavation work — identifies hazards and has authority to act
- Protection required at 5 feet — but unstable soil may require protection at any depth
- Soil classification: Type A (strongest) → Type C (weakest) — determines maximum allowable slope
- Three options: sloping, shoring, trench shield — each has specific requirements
- Call 811 before excavating — spoil minimum 2 feet from edge — access every 25 feet

questions.q: Under OSHA 29 CFR 1926 Subpart P, at what depth must protective systems be provided for most excavations?
questions.explanation: Protective systems are required for excavations 5 feet or deeper. However, even at shallower depths, a competent person who determines unstable soil is present must require protective systems.

questions.q: What is the maximum allowable slope for a Type C soil excavation?
questions.explanation: Type C soils (granular, sandy, submerged, or weakest cohesive) require the flattest slope — 1.5:1 (34°). This represents the highest risk soil and requires the most generous cut-back for sloped excavations.

questions.q: A trench box (trench shield) is installed in an excavation. What does it protect against?
options: It prevents the trench walls from collapsing
It protects workers inside the shield if a collapse occurs — it does NOT prevent collapse
It prevents groundwater from entering the trench
It acts as shoring and supports the full soil load
questions.explanation: A trench box (shield) does NOT prevent soil collapse — it protects workers inside the shield zone if a collapse occurs. Workers must remain within the protected area. It is a protective system, not a shoring system.

questions.q: How close to the excavation edge may excavated spoil be placed under OSHA Subpart P?
options: Right at the edge — no restriction
questions.explanation: OSHA requires excavated spoil to be placed a minimum of 2 feet from the excavation edge. Spoil placed closer creates surcharge loading on the wall and increases collapse risk.

questions.q: What is the maximum distance from any worker in an excavation deeper than 4 feet to an access/egress point (ladder or ramp)?
questions.explanation: OSHA requires a ladder, stairway, or ramp within 25 feet of workers in excavations deeper than 4 feet. In an emergency, workers must be able to evacuate quickly — 25 feet is the maximum lateral travel distance to an access point.

lessons.title: GHS, Safety Data Sheets, Labelling and Right-to-Know
lessons.content:
## OSHA Hazard Communication Standard — 29 CFR 1910.1200 (HazCom 2012)

The OSHA Hazard Communication Standard (HCS), updated in 2012 to align with the Globally Harmonized System (GHS), ensures workers have the information needed to protect themselves from chemical hazards. It applies to both general industry AND construction.

## The Right-to-Know

HazCom 2012 is built on the worker's **right to know** about chemical hazards:
- Every worker who may be exposed to hazardous chemicals has the right to information
- Employers must provide information through labels, SDSs, and training
- This right cannot be waived or restricted

## GHS — Globally Harmonized System

GHS is an international framework for classifying and communicating chemical hazards. OSHA's HazCom 2012 adopted GHS to create consistency with global trading partners (Canada, EU, Australia).

**GHS Hazard Classes:**
- Physical hazards: Flammable, explosive, oxidising, compressed gas
- Health hazards: Acute toxicity, carcinogenicity, reproductive toxicity, STOT
- Environmental hazards: Aquatic toxicity (informational in US — not enforced by OSHA)

**GHS Pictograms (9 symbols):**
| Pictogram | Hazard |
|-----------|-------|
| Flame | Flammable, self-reactive, pyrophoric |
| Flame over circle | Oxidizer |
| Exploding bomb | Explosive |
| Skull and crossbones | Acute toxicity (fatal/toxic) |
| Exclamation mark | Irritant, harmful, narcotic, sensitizer |
| Health hazard | Carcinogen, mutagen, reproductive toxin, STOT, aspiration |
| Corrosion | Skin/eye corrosion, metal corrosion |
| Gas cylinder | Compressed gases |
| Environment | Aquatic toxicity |

## Safety Data Sheets (SDS) — 16 Sections

The SDS provides comprehensive hazard and safe handling information:

| Section | Content |
|---------|---------|
| 1 | Identification |
| 2 | Hazard(s) identification |
| 3 | Composition/ingredients |
| 4 | First-aid measures |
| 5 | Fire-fighting measures |
| 6 | Accidental release measures |
| 7 | Handling and storage |
| 8 | Exposure controls/PPE |
| 9 | Physical and chemical properties |
| 10–16 | Stability, toxicology, ecological, disposal, transport, regulatory, other |

**SDS access requirements:**
- Must be readily accessible to workers during each work shift
- In the work area where chemicals are used
- Electronic access acceptable if always available and no barriers

## Label Requirements

HazCom 2012 labels must include:
1. Product identifier
2. Signal word (DANGER or WARNING)
3. Hazard statements (describe nature and degree of hazard)
4. Precautionary statements (prevention, response, storage, disposal)
5. GHS pictogram(s)
6. Supplier information (name, address, phone)

**Workplace labels (secondary containers):**
When chemicals are transferred to secondary containers, a workplace label must be applied. Minimum: product name and words, pictures, or symbols for hazards.

## Chemical Inventory and Written Program

Employers must maintain:
- A **written HazCom program** describing how labels, SDSs, and training are managed
- A **chemical inventory list** of all hazardous chemicals used in the workplace
- SDSs for every chemical on the inventory

## Worker Training Requirements

Training must be provided:
- Before initial assignment to work with hazardous chemicals
- Whenever a new hazard is introduced
- Training content: how to read labels and SDSs, specific chemical hazards in their work area, protective measures, and emergency procedures

## Key Takeaways

- HazCom 2012: GHS-aligned — standardised pictograms, signal words, 16-section SDSs
- 9 GHS pictograms — skull/crossbones = acute fatal/toxic; exclamation = irritant/harmful
- SDS: 16 sections — Section 2 (hazards) and Section 8 (exposure controls/PPE) most critical for workers
- Labels: 6 required elements — signal word (DANGER/WARNING), pictograms, hazard and precautionary statements
- Written HazCom program + chemical inventory required for every construction employer

questions.q: What signal word is used on GHS labels for the more severe hazard category?
questions.explanation: GHS uses two signal words: DANGER for more severe hazards and WARNING for less severe hazards. These words appear prominently on labels and alert workers to the general level of hazard before reading full details.

questions.q: Which GHS pictogram represents an acute toxicity hazard (fatal or toxic if swallowed, inhaled, or absorbed)?
questions.explanation: The skull and crossbones pictogram indicates acute toxicity — substances that are fatal or toxic in small amounts. The exclamation mark indicates less severe acute toxicity (harmful), irritants, or sensitisers.

questions.q: Under HazCom 2012, when must worker training on hazardous chemicals be provided?
options: Annually at a scheduled safety day
Before initial assignment and whenever a new hazard is introduced to the work area
questions.explanation: Training must be provided before initial assignment to work with hazardous chemicals AND whenever a new hazardous chemical or new hazard is introduced. This ensures workers always have current, relevant information.

questions.q: What does Section 8 of a Safety Data Sheet (SDS) contain?
questions.explanation: SDS Section 8 contains Exposure Controls and PPE — permissible exposure limits (PELs), threshold limit values (TLVs), engineering controls, and specific PPE requirements for safe handling. This section is critical for workers selecting protective measures.

lessons.title: 29 CFR 1926.95 — Hard Hats, Eye Protection and Foot Protection
lessons.content:
## OSHA PPE Requirements for Construction — 29 CFR 1926.95

Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) requirements for construction are established under 29 CFR 1926.95 (criteria for PPE) and specific subparts for head, eye, face, foot, and hand protection. PPE is the last line of defence in the hierarchy of controls.

## Employer Responsibilities for PPE

Under 29 CFR 1926.95(a), the employer must provide PPE **at no cost to the employee** when:
- Engineering and administrative controls are not feasible or sufficient
- While engineering controls are being installed
- For certain PPE that is not personal use (safety glasses, hard hats, etc.)

**Employer must also:**
- Assess the workplace to determine PPE needs (PPE Hazard Assessment — certify in writing)
- Select PPE that fits properly
- Train employees on when/how to use PPE, maintenance, and limitations
- Replace worn or damaged PPE

## Head Protection — 29 CFR 1926.100

Hard hats (safety helmets) are required when workers are exposed to:
- Falling objects
- Electrical shock and burns
- Bumping into fixed objects

**ANSI/ISEA Z89.1 Classes:**
| Class | Protection |
|-------|-----------|
| **Class E (Electrical)** | Protects against electrical hazards up to 20,000 volts |
| **Class G (General)** | General impact — 2,200 volts electrical protection |
| **Class C (Conductive)** | Impact only — no electrical protection |

**Types:**
- Type I: Top impact protection only
- Type II: Top and lateral (side) impact protection — required in some high-risk applications

**Maintenance:**
- Inspect before each use — cracks, dents, penetrations, fading
- Replace after significant impact — even if no visible damage
- Replace every 5 years (shell) / 1–2 years (suspension) or per manufacturer
- Do not paint, drill, or apply adhesive labels — compromises integrity

## Eye and Face Protection — 29 CFR 1926.102

Eye protection required when workers are exposed to:
- Flying particles (grinding, chipping, nailing)
- Molten metal (welding)
- Chemical splashes
- Harmful light radiation
- High-pressure liquid or gas

**ANSI/ISEA Z87.1 standard — types:**
- Safety glasses: side shields required when flying object hazard exists
- Chemical goggles: sealed against liquid splashes
- Face shield: protects face from splashes and large debris — worn over safety glasses
- Welding helmets/goggles: shade lenses selected by process (shade 3–14)

**Prescription glasses:** Regular prescription glasses do NOT meet OSHA requirements. Workers must use prescription safety glasses or safety glasses worn over contacts/prescription.

## Foot Protection — 29 CFR 1926.96

Safety footwear required when workers are exposed to:
- Falling or rolling objects
- Sharp objects that could puncture the sole
- Electrical hazards
- Hot surfaces, molten metal splatter

**ASTM F2413 standard:**
- Impact resistance (75 or 50 ft-lb toe cap rating)
- Compression resistance
- Metatarsal protection (for high-risk drop hazards)
- Puncture-resistant sole plate
- Electrical hazard (EH) rating where needed

## Hand Protection

OSHA requires hand protection where workers' hands are exposed to hazards such as:
- Skin absorption of harmful substances
- Cuts, lacerations from sharp materials
- Extreme temperatures
- Chemical burns

Glove selection must match the specific hazard — chemical resistance ratings vary by glove material.

## PPE Hazard Assessment

OSHA requires employers to:
1. Survey the workplace for hazards
2. Determine PPE needed for each hazard
3. **Certify the assessment in writing** (written certification document)
4. Select appropriate ANSI-rated PPE
5. Train workers

## Key Takeaways

- Employer provides PPE at no cost — except for personal-use items in limited cases
- Written PPE hazard assessment required — must be certified in writing
- Hard hats: Class E for electrical, Class G for general, Class C for impact only — replace after any significant impact
- Eye protection: ANSI Z87.1 — safety glasses with side shields, goggles for chemical splash, face shield over glasses
- Safety footwear: ASTM F2413 — impact, compression, puncture resistance, EH rating where required

questions.q: Under OSHA 29 CFR 1926.95, who is responsible for providing PPE to construction workers?
options: Workers must provide their own PPE
The employer must provide PPE at no cost to the employee in most situations
The general contractor provides PPE to employees of subcontractors
PPE is provided by OSHA through compliance assistance programs
questions.explanation: Employers must provide required PPE at no cost to employees under 29 CFR 1926.95. The only exceptions involve certain personal-use PPE (like some footwear and non-specialty safety glasses) where cost-sharing may apply under specific conditions.

questions.q: What ANSI Class of hard hat provides protection against electrical hazards up to 20,000 volts?
questions.explanation: Class E (Electrical) hard hats protect against electrical hazards up to 20,000 volts. Class G (General) provides 2,200 volt protection. Class C (Conductive) provides no electrical protection — impact only.

questions.q: A worker needs eye protection while using a grinder. Which combination provides the best protection?
options: ANSI Z87.1 safety glasses with side shields
ANSI Z87.1 safety glasses with side shields plus a face shield over them
questions.explanation: For grinding, both safety glasses AND a face shield provide the best protection — the face shield protects against larger debris and sparks while the safety glasses protect if the face shield is lifted. Regular or prescription glasses do not meet OSHA requirements.

questions.q: OSHA requires employers to certify a PPE hazard assessment. What form does this certification take?
options: A verbal confirmation at the toolbox talk
A written certification document identifying the workplace evaluated, date, the person certifying, and that the assessment was performed
An annual licence renewal with the state regulator
questions.explanation: OSHA requires a written certification of the PPE hazard assessment — a document identifying the workplace evaluated, the date of the assessment, the person who performed it, and certification that a hazard assessment was conducted. This is a documentation requirement.

questions.q: Which ASTM standard governs safety footwear for construction workers in the United States?
questions.explanation: ASTM F2413 is the standard for performance requirements for protective (safety) footwear — covering impact resistance, compression, puncture resistance, metatarsal protection, and electrical hazard ratings. ANSI Z87.1 covers eye protection, ANSI Z89.1 covers head protection.
